summary: Typosquat of safer-buffer ships hidden staged loader that spawns detached node child on require
details: This package is a scope-namespaced lookalike of the legitimate `safer-buffer` Buffer polyfill. Its main module `safer.js` contains, alongside the benign polyfill code, a hidden loader block wrapped in a bare `try {... } catch(error) {}`. On require, the loader walks up to the host project's `package.json`, checks a target-fingerprint condition (`pkg.dependencies.express == '^5.2.1'`), and — if the companion file `tests/safer-buffer.min.js` is present — spawns a detached `node` child process to execute that file with `spawn("node", [specialPath, "safer-buffer"], {detached:true,...})`. Persistence state (the child PID) is smuggled into the last line of `Porting-Buffer.md` and read back to gate respawns. A Buffer polyfill has no legitimate reason to inspect the consumer project's dependency versions, hide state inside a markdown document, silently swallow errors, or launch a detached node process against a sibling file that is not part of the polyfill API. The concealment shape (target-project fingerprinting, silent-catch, PID hidden in an unrelated doc, dropper block inserted between benign setup and the exported API) is characteristic of a staged supply-chain loader, and the sibling file executes attacker-controlled JavaScript in the installer's Node runtime as soon as any consumer requires the package.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
